NSA Neuroanatomy — 6-Month Study Plan
Part-time clinical schedule · 3–4 hrs/day · Strong existing base · Target 95%+
Daily structure — 3.5 hrs (Mon–Sat)
Monday — new material
0–25 minAnki due cards — zero skipping
25–35 minBrain dump: blank page, recall everything from last session
35–95 minNew material — deep read of Last's, one topic section
95–125 minDraw key structures from memory — no peeking
125–185 minDifferent topic (interleaving) — brief active recall session
185–210 minAdd new Anki cards from today's material
Tuesday — retrieval + new
0–25 minAnki due cards
25–45 minRetrieval test on Monday's material — blank paper only
45–115 minNew material — next section
115–175 minClinical application: draw the lesion, explain the deficit
175–210 minAdd Anki cards
Wednesday — interleaved retrieval
0–25 minAnki due cards
25–85 minTopic A retrieval (from 2 weeks ago)
85–145 minTopic B retrieval (from 1 week ago)
145–210 minTopic C — new material or further depth
Thursday — drawing & spatial
0–25 minAnki due cards
25–95 minDrawing session: brainstem cross-sections, CoW, spinal cord — all from memory
95–155 minNew material — next section of weekly topic
155–210 minTeach-back: explain today's topic out loud without notes
Friday — clinical vignettes
0–25 minAnki due cards
25–125 minClinical anatomy vignettes — applied questions spanning multiple topics
125–175 minReview wrong answers — trace back to Last's, fix the gap
175–210 minAdd cards for anything that tripped you up
Saturday — consolidation
0–25 minAnki due cards
25–85 minFull week brain dump — blank page, everything from this week's topic
85–145 minFill gaps identified in brain dump from Last's
145–210 minPlan next week: read chapter headings, prime your brain
Sunday — rest (non-negotiable)
15–20 minAnki due cards only — nothing else. Protect your consolidation.
Anki targets across 6 months
177
cards now
300+
target by week 8
30
min daily
How to use your existing deck
1
Start reviewing the 177 cards immediately — even before you begin Phase 1. Spaced repetition only works if you start the clock now.
2
Use the tags to study in parallel with your weekly topic. During Week 1 (spinal cord), filter to CNS_SpinalCord and CNS_Pathways cards daily.
3
Add new cards weekly as you go deeper into Last's. Target 10–15 new cards per study day during Phases 1–3, then stop adding in Phase 4+.
4
Honesty rule: if you cannot produce the answer without peeking, it is a fail. Never rate a card Good on recognition alone.
5
From Week 12 onwards, move to free-recall mode for older topics: cover your Anki deck and do one blank-page brain dump per topic per week.
6
In the final 4 weeks, do not add any new cards. Only review mature cards. Trust the algorithm.
Card types to add as you study
A
Forward cards: "What are the contents of the superior orbital fissure?" — fact retrieval.
B
Reverse cards: "Wrist drop + minimal sensory loss — nerve, level of injury, mechanism?" — clinical to anatomy.
C
Drawing cards: "Draw the medulla at the level of the inferior olive from memory" — spatial retrieval.
D
Why cards: "Why does the facial nerve loop over the abducent nucleus rather than passing straight through?" — mechanistic understanding.
Progress checkpoints
End wk 3Can draw all 7 brainstem cross-sections from memory and name every nucleus visible at each levelSelf-test
End wk 6Can trace any cranial nerve from nucleus to periphery and state the clinical deficit at each possible lesion pointSelf-test
End wk 8Anki deck at 300+ cards, all maturing. Can reproduce Circle of Willis with all named branches and vascular territory deficitsSelf-test
End wk 12Can take any clinical vignette and localise the lesion anatomically, citing Last's page-level detailVignette
End wk 18Full coverage of Last's neuroanatomy and head/neck complete. Every Anki tag has mature cards. Peripheral nerve lesion patterns fluent.Coverage
Wk 22First full timed mock exam. Target score ≥80%. Adjust Phase 5 plan to weight identified gaps more heavily.Mock
Wk 25Second full mock exam + oral viva with a colleague. Target score ≥90%. Anki mature card review only from this point.Mock
Wk 26Exam. Sleep 8 hours every night this week. Stop all study 24 hours before. You have done the work.NSA exam
Topic coverage tracker
Brainstem & cranial nervesWks 2–5
Spinal cord & pathwaysWk 1 + 9
Cerebral hemispheresWk 6 + 10
Vascular supplyWk 8
Ventricular system & CSFWk 7
Head & neck (Ch. 6)Wks 13–17
Upper limb peripheral nervesWk 18
Lower limb peripheral nervesWk 19
CNS developmentWk 12
Clinical integration & mocksWks 20–26
The 8 non-negotiables
1
Anki every single day — including Sundays, holidays, and bad days. Missing even one day compounds forward debt into the scheduling algorithm. 15 minutes on the worst days.
2
8 hours sleep. This is not optional. Slow-wave sleep is when anatomy consolidates. A 6-hour night erases approximately 40% of the day's encoding. Sleep is study time.
3
Never re-read as a primary method. Every time you reach for Last's, ask: "Am I reading to learn something new, or avoiding the discomfort of retrieval?" If the latter, close the book and draw instead.
4
No new material in the final 4 weeks. Confidence in exam conditions comes from depth on what you know, not breadth of what you've glanced at.
5
Do at least one drawing per day from memory. Spatial anatomy cannot be learned from text alone. Your hand must reconstruct it.
6
Clinical application every week from week 1 — not week 12. Every anatomical structure has a clinical consequence. If you can't name the deficit in week 1, you won't own it in the exam.
7
Sunday is protected rest. 6 high-quality days outperforms 7 depleted ones. Do your Anki cards, then stop. No guilt, no exceptions.
8
Track your weak spots honestly. Keep a running list of topics you struggled with in vignettes and mocks. Phase 5 exists entirely to attack that list.
What 95%+ actually requires
✓
Being able to draw every brainstem cross-section level from memory and name every visible nucleus and tract — not recognise from a diagram.
✓
Tracing every cranial nerve from its nucleus to its peripheral distribution in under 2 minutes, with lesion consequences at each level.
✓
Explaining the embryological basis for the arrangement of brainstem nuclei — not just knowing the positions, but why they are where they are.
✓
Knowing the surgical relations of the thyroid gland, the recurrent laryngeal nerve, and the contents of the cavernous sinus as fluently as your own address.
✓
Being able to take any clinical vignette and construct the anatomical answer from first principles, not from memorised question templates.
